coredata.dc:title: Nanomaterials for fluorescent detection of curcumin
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Yongming
Yang, Chao
Zhang, Yijia
Tao, Tao
coredata.dc:description: Owing to the attractive biological and pharmacological activities, sensitive and selective detection of curcumin is of great significance. Nanomaterials possessing unique optical properties exhibit potential applications in the fluorescent detection of curcumin. This review first discussed the detection strategies of fluorescent nanosensors. In the subsequent section, we highlighted the recent advances of different nanomaterials for fluorescent detection of curcumin, including semiconductor QDs, lanthanide upconversion nanoparticles, fluorescent metal nanoclusters, and carbon quantum dots. And we further provided the merits of fluorescent nanosensors for curcumin. Lastly, the challenges and further directions were presented.

Fluorescent
Detection
Curcumin
Advances

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection strategies of fluorescent nanosensors for curcumin
Merits of fluorescent nanosensors for curcumin

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of curcumin, inset: pictures of curcumin under visible and UV light. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of curcumin under different excitation wavelengths. Reprinted with permission from [22].

(A) Illustration of preparation of lanthanide UCNPs and the fluorescence turn-off detection of curcumin with lanthanide UCNPs. Reprinted with permission from [37]. (B) Scheme of synthesis of urazole-F-AuNCs and urazole-F-AuNCs-based nanosensors for fluorescence turn-off determination of curcumin. Reprinted with permission from [44].

(A) Scheme of synthesis of PVP-F-CuNCs and the interaction between PVP-F-CuNCs and curcumin. Reprinted with permission from [23]. (B) Schematic illustration of preparation of CQDs and the detection principle for curcumin based on CQDs by IFE and dynamic quenching process. Reprinted with permission from [26].

(A) Illustration of nitrogen-doped CQDs for fluorescence turn-off assay of curcumin. Reprinted with permission from [58]. (B) Schematic explanation of synthesis of red emissive CQDs and the fluorescence turn-off determination of curcumin with the CQDs. Reprinted with permission from [61].

(A) Scheme of the synthesis of nitrogen and chlorine-doped CQDs and fluorescence turn-off detection of curcumin. Reprinted with permission from [21]. (B) Illustration of the synthetic procedure of (Gd,Eu)2O3-PEI@CQDs and fluorescence turn-off determination of curcumin. Reprinted with permission from [29].
Comparison the performance of electrochemical method, liquid chromatographic method, organic fluorescent probe and fluorescent nanosensors for sensing curcumin.
Comparison of the performance of nanosensors for fluorescent detection of curcumin.

head.ce:title: Nanomaterials for fluorescent detection of curcumin

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

Nanjing University of Information Science & Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Changwang School of Honors, Nanjing University of Information Science & Technology, Nanjing 210044, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Owing to the attractive biological and pharmacological activities, sensitive and selective detection of curcumin is of great significance. Nanomaterials possessing unique optical properties exhibit potential applications in the fluorescent detection of curcumin. This review first discussed the detection strategies of fluorescent nanosensors. In the subsequent section, we highlighted the recent advances of different nanomaterials for fluorescent detection of curcumin, including semiconductor QDs, lanthanide upconversion nanoparticles, fluorescent metal nanoclusters, and carbon quantum dots. And we further provided the merits of fluorescent nanosensors for curcumin. Lastly, the challenges and further directions were presented.

ce:section.ce:para: Curcumin, found in rhizomes of turmeric [1,2], is a natural polyphenol compounds. And curcumin exhibited attractive physiological and pharmacological activities including anticancer [3,4], antiviral [3,5], anti-inflammatory [4,6,7], antioxidation [7,8], antibacterial [9,10], anti-epilepsy activities [11] and so on. Meanwhile, curcumin is also widely used in the foodstuff industry as a yellow pigment [12]. And World Health Organization recommends the daily dosage of curcumin as a food additive is 0–3 mg/kg [13]. Therefore, development of facile and sensitive approaches for sensing curcumin is highly desirable. Up to now, considerable methods have been applied for detection of curcumin, such as electrochemical methods [14], liquid chromatography [15,16], UV–Vis spectrophotometry [17,18], fluorescence methods [19,20] and so on. Among these methods, fluorescent detection method is highly desirable due to its ease of operation, good sensitivity and rapid response.

Recently, various emerging nanomaterials have been employed for constructing fluorescent nanosensors for curcumin, including semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), lanthanide upconversion nanoparticles (UCNPs), fluorescent metal nanoclusters (F-MNCs) and carbon quantum dots (CQDs). However, there were no reviews on this topic. It is therefore urgently to provide a review on the recent development of various nanomaterials for fluorescent sensing curcumin, which will present useful information for further researches in the related field.

In this article, we first discussed the general detection strategies of fluorescent nanosensors for curcumin. In the next section, we highlighted the recent advances of various nanomaterials for fluorescent determination of curcumin. In the subsequent section, the merits of fluorescent nanosensors for curcumin are provided. Lastly, we presented the chances and challenges about the fluorescent nanosensors of curcumin.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection strategies of fluorescent nanosensors for curcumin
ce:section.ce:para: Up to now, there are mainly two fluorescence detection strategies: fluorescence turn-off and ratiometric fluorescence response. In the fluorescence turn-off mode, inner filter effect (IFE), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), static quenching and dynamic quenching process can contribute to the fluorescence quenching. For the IFE, when the absorption spectrum of curcumin partially or totally overlaps with the excitation or emission spectra of nanomaterials, resulting in the fluorescence quenching of nanomaterials because curcumin can adsorb the energy of the excitation or emission light. And FRET may contribute to the fluorescence quenching, which can be verified by the change of fluorescence lifetime [23,24]. In the FRET mode, curcumin as an energy acceptor can quench the fluorescence of nanomaterials acting as energy donor. Moreover, other interactions-caused static quenching and dynamic quenching process can also induce the fluorescence quenching. In the static quenching process, curcumin and fluorescent nanomaterials have a stacking interaction to form nonfluorescent ground-state complexes. In the presence of curcumin, the changed absorption spectrum of the fluorescent nanomaterials and constant fluorescence lifetime are related with the static quenching process. However, the dynamic quenching process is associated with the collision between curcumin and fluorescent nanomaterials in the excited state. The dynamic quenching process can be verified by the invariable absorption spectrum of fluorescent nanomaterials and changed fluorescence lifetime in the presence of curcumin. Moreover, in many instances, combined static quenching and dynamic quenching process contribute fluorescence quenching [25–27]. Notably, the fluorescence signal of fluorescence turn-off detection system is easily affected by the change of experimental conditions, probably generating incorrect results. For the ratiometric fluorescence response, the nanomaterials exhibit dual emissions, curcumin can selectively quench one emission band due to photo-induced electron transfer, IFE or FRET [28,29]. Furthermore, curcumin exhibits yellow fluorescence, which can be used to establish ratiometric fluorescence nanosensors with fluorescent nanomaterials. In the ratiometric fluorescence nanosensors, the addition of curcumin causes the decline of the fluorescence of nanomaterials and increase of the yellow fluorescence of curcumin. Based on the fluorescence ratio changes, determination of curcumin is achieved [22]. The ratiometric fluorescent detection system can rule out the incorrect results caused by the change of experimental conditions. It is worth deeply exploring the ratiometric fluorescent nanosensors of curcumin.

In the past decades, considerable efforts have been devoted to exploring synthesis and applications of various nanomaterials. And a variety of fluorescent nanomaterials have been employed for sensing curcumin. In the following section, we mainly presented the recent progress of various fluorescent nanosensors of curcumin.

Semiconductor QDs have aroused considerable interest due to their high quantum yield and tunable emission behavior [30]. Mn-doped ZnS QDs with low toxicity have been widely utilized for establishment of various nanosensors [31]. Shuang’s group synthesized 3-mercaptopropionic acid stabilized Mn-doped ZnS QDs (MPA-Mn-ZnS QDs) with dual emissions centered at 458 nm and 590 nm. Interestingly, curcumin was observed to lead to the defect-related fluorescence quenching of MPA-Mn-ZnS QDs at 458 nm, while the emission at 590 nm remained stable, which was attributed to the spectroscopic photo-induced electron transfer and FRET. Accordingly, selective and sensitive ratiometric detection of curcumin based on MPA-Mn-ZnS QDs was accomplished with a linear relationship in the range of 0.16–16.9 μM and a limit of detection (LOD) of 37 nM. The as-prepared MPA-Mn-ZnS QDs displayed excellent specificity towards curcumin over some common ions, small organic molecules and various amino acids. And urine samples were utilized to evaluate the feasibility of the approach, revealing the potential applications of the proposed method [28]. However, great efforts are still required to improve the robustness of the method.

Lanthanide UCNPs, newly emerging fluorescent nanomaterials, have attracted great attentions because of tunable emission properties, high quantum yield and good stability [32,33]. Thus, plenty of lanthanide UCNPs have been applied in many fields, such as biomedicine, solar cells, security encoding [34], bioimaging [35], biosensing [36] and so on. Similarly, PEI-modified core/shell β-NaYF4:Tm3+,Yb3+@NaYF4 UCNPs were prepared for fluorescence turn-off determination of curcumin via FRET. In the process, curcumin was easily close to the core/shell UCNPs through electrostatic interactions. Meanwhile the formation of a Meisenheimer complex between the benzene ring of curcumin and the amino group on the surface of UCNPs could adsorb the fluorescence of UCNPs (Fig. 3 A). Notably, other chemicals with benzene rings, including trimesic acid, 2,4-dinitrobenzoic acid, benzene, 1-methyl-4-nitrosobenzene, 4-nitroanisole, methylbenzene, dibutyl phthalate and nitrobenzene, did not interfere with the detection of curcumin, revealing the good selectivity of the developed approach. As a result, a good linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity of UCNPs at 474 nm and the curcumin concentration of 0.625–3.125 ng/mL was obtained with a LOD of 0.625 ng/mL. At the same time, the proposed method was also used to detect other curcuminoids (curcumin-alanine and curcumin-moroxydine) with similar performance, indicating the promising applications of the developed approach [37]. Yet more novel lanthanide UCNPs-based nanosensors of curcumin are still highly desirable.

Compared to semiconductor QDs and lanthanide UCNPs, F-MNCs including fluorescent gold nanoclusters (F-AuNCs), fluorescent silver nanoclusters (F-AgNCs) and fluorescent copper nanoclusters (F-CuNCs) exhibited good photostability, ease of fabrication and subnanometer size, which have been explored in biomedicine, sensors, catalysis, bioimaging and so on [38–43]. And F-AuNCs were also used for fluorescent determination of curcumin. Yang et al. have proposed a one-step approach for preparing urazole-F-AuNCs with urazole and polyvinyl pyrrolidone as templates. The as-synthesized urazole-F-AuNCs showed a maximum emission centered at 473 nm with the excitation at 381 nm, as a result, the excitation and emission spectra of urazole-F-AuNCs overlapped with the UV–vis absorption spectrum of curcumin, causing the fluorescence quenching of urazole-F-AuNCs (Fig. 3B). Consequently, fluorescence turn-off detection of curcumin was realized on the basis of urazole-F-AuNCs. And a linear relationship in the range of 1–60 μM and a LOD of 72 nM were obtained. And some common ions, natural metal coordination ligands, styrene and the coexistent substances in curry powder showed negligible effect on the fluorescence emission spectra of urazole-F-AuNCs, suggesting the excellent selectivity of urazole-F-AuNCs towards curcumin. Furthermore, the developed method was successfully applied to determine curcumin in curry powder with satisfactory results [44].

F-AgNCs, a type of fluorescent metal nanoclusters, have attracted great interests due to ease of fabrication, photostability and subnanometer size [42].Recently, Cai et al. developed an approach for fluorescence turn-off assay of curcumin using polyethyleneimine-stabilized F-AgNCs (PEI-F-AgNCs). The PEI-F-AgNCs were prepared via the reduction of AgNO3 with ascorbic acid at room temperature. The as-prepared PEI-F-AgNCs exhibited green emission centered at about 510 nm, which was easily quenched by curcumin due to IFE and static quenching, yielding the selective detection of curcumin with linear range of 0.1–100 μM and a LOD of 0.027 μM. And some potential interfering substances including some metal ions and biological molecules did not interfere with the detection of curcumin, demonstrating the good selectivity of the proposed method. Furthermore, the feasibility of the developed probes was evaluated in bovine serum samples, and satisfactory results were achieved [45].

Compared with F-AuNCs and F-AgNCs, F-CuNCs possess low-cost and adjustable surface functionalization with considerable ligands [43]. Recently, F-CuNCs have been utilized for fluorescence turn-off sensing curcumin. In the work, Yao and co-workers first prepared polyvinyl pyrrolidone-templated F-CuNCs (PVP-F-CuNCs) at 90 °C. The as-prepared PVP-F-CuNCs exhibited a maximum emission wavelength at 510 nm under excitation of 380 nm. And the wide UV–vis absorption spectrum of curcumin overlapped with the excitation and emission spectra of PVP-F-CuNCs, yielding FRET between PVP-F-CuNCs and curcumin, and easily decreasing the fluorescence of PVP-F-CuNCs (Fig. 4 A). Based on the fluorescence turn-off mechanism, sensitive assay of curcumin was successfully built with a linear range of 0.1–10 μg/mL and a LOD of 21 ng/mL. And some common saccharides, metal ions, amino acids, antibiotics and anticarcinogens exhibited no obvious interferences with the detection of curcumin, demonstrating good selectivity of the proposed method. Importantly, the practicability of the proposed approach was evaluated in human serum with excellent recovery [23].

CQDs are novel type fluorescence nanomaterials with attractive properties of low-cost, good photostability, high quantum yield, good water solubility, low toxicity, ease of preparation, excellent biocompatibility and so on. Thus, CQDs have been demonstrated in various fields including sensing [24,46–48], energy [49], catalysis [48,49], biomedicine [47,50], bioimaging [48,50], optoelectronics [50] and so on. In recent years, various CQDs have been demonstrated to detect curcumin. Chen’s group synthesized blue-emissive CQDs with average size of 3.3 ± 0.4 nm and the quantum yield of ∼43% via one-step thermal treatment of chicken egg white. The obtained CQDs were utilized for fluorescence turn-off detection of curcumin through FRET. As a result, a good linear range ofof 0.78–50 μM and LOD of 40 nM were achieved. And some environmental and biologically relevant molecules including cysteine, glutamine, histidine and lysine displayed inconspicuous impact on the assay of curcumin, indicating the good selectivity of the developed method. Importantly, the proposed approach was successfully applied for sensing curcumin in complex turmeric powder and condensed turmeric tablets. Moreover, the as-prepared CQDs have been used to label Staphylococcus aureus and Escherichia coli [51]. Alsobluish green emissive CQDs fabricated through hydrothermal treatment of m-phenylenediamine and citric acid were applied for selective fluorescence turn-off detection of curcumin via IFE and dynamic interaction process (Fig. 4B).Under the optimal conditions, a good linearity ranging from 0.01 μM to 25.0 μM and a LOD of 28.7 nM were accomplished. And different kinds of anions, cations, amino acids and small molecules displayed negligible influence on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs, suggesting the good selectivity of the proposed method. Moreover, the feasibility of the developed method was evaluated in dietary food samples with excellent recovery [26]. CQDs with blue emission at 420 nm could be also fabricated into a ratiometric probe for sensing curcumin. In the process, curcumin exhibited fluorescence centered at 525 nm, the addition of curcumin could enhance the fluorescence intensity at 525 nm and simultaneously reduce the fluorescence intensity at 420 nm due to IFE. As consequence, a good linear relationship between ratiometric fluorescence intensity (I525/I420) and the curcumin concentration in the region of 5–30 μM was established. And the LOD was 21.79 nM. And tyrosine, alanine, phenylalanine, cysteine, threonine, glutathione, doxycycline, oxytetracycline, primiamycin, roxithromycin, ofloxacin and clarithromycin were demonstrated to show no obvious influence on the fluorescence spectra of the CQDs, manifesting the good selectivity of the proposed method. Moreover, the CQDs-curcumin was able to ratiometrically sense Fe3+ via the selective interaction of Fe3+ and curcumin [22]. Yu et al. prepared blue emissive CQDs with up-conversion fluorescence properties via the solvothermal treatment of p-aminobenzoic acid and ethanol. The as-prepared CQDs could be utilized for fluorescence turn-off detection of curcumin based on IFE. They observed a good linearity between the fluorescence intensity and the curcumin concentration ranging from 0.4 µΜ to 45 µΜ. And the LOD was 0.133 µM. And the developed method showed good selectivity towards for curcumin because some amino acids, drugs and other potential interfering substances exhibited no obvious impact on fluorescence spectra of the CQDs. Furthermore, the approach was successfully applied to determine curcumin content in curry powder with high accuracy and good recovery, demonstrating the potential applications of the developed method [52].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), another type of CQDs, were also used to assay curcumin. Pinilla-Peñalver and co-workers recently found that GQDs could recognize free curcumin and nanocurcumin via different fluorescence turn-off behaviors. The gradual addition of free curcumin could cause a gradual quenching and a red-shifting from 458 to 490 nm with excitation wavelength at 356 nm, which was ascribed to the hydrogen-bond and π-stacking interactions between curcumin and GQDs. However, nanocurcumin could only quench the fluorescence of GQDs. As a consequence, linear ranges of 1.0–25.0 and 0.3–4.5 μg/mL for detection of free curcumin and nanocurcumin were obtained with LOD of 0.3 and 0.1 μg/mL. And some potential interfering substances such as omega-3, glucose, sucrose, lactose, vitamin D and SDS, and cations including K+, Ca2+, and Na+ did not interfere with curcumin detection. Importantly, the GQDs could quantify and distinguish between free curcumin and nanocurcumin in ginger tea [53]. Tade and Patil presented a green approach for synthesizing blue-emitting GQDs via the hydrothermal treatment of cellulose nanocrystals derived from bamboo timber waste. They found that the GQDs could be applied for selective fluorescence turn-off detection of curcumin with a linear range of 0–1.0 μM and a LOD of 30.07 nM, which was attributable to the surface protonation and FRET effect. And some common metal ions and other biochemicals showed little influence on the detection system, indicating the high selectivity of the GQDs towards curcumin. Additionally, the developed method was successfully applied to detect curcumin in ginger juice with good recovery [54]. However, the linear range of the proposed approach was too narrow. And GQDs derived from the carbonization of L-cysteine were applied for fluorescence turn-off detection of curcumin based on IFE. Furthermore, other biomolecules and metal ions did not interfere with the detection of curcumin. Disappointingly, linear range and LOD was not obtained [55].

Heteroatoms doping may be an effective approach to improve the optical properties of CQDs [56,57], as a result, many heteroatom-doped CQDs have been fabricated to detect curcumin. A kind of nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) with an average size of 5.23 nm from hydrothermal treatment of citric acid and urea showed good photostability and a high quantum yield of 25.4%. And the addition of curcumin could quench the fluorescence of the resultant N-CQDs, which was attributed to the IFE (Fig. 5 A). Accordingly, sensitive assay of curcumin was accomplished with a linear range of 0.2–10 μM and a LOD of 84.8 nM. And other common ions and related compounds did not interfere with the detection of curcumin. Importantly, the feasibility of the proposed approach was demonstrated in urine samples [58]. Bu et al. have prepared N-CQDs through the hydrothermal treatment of chrysanthemum and ethylenediamine. The as-prepared N-CQDs exhibited excellent photostability and good biocompatibility, making the N-CQDs to be utilized for cell imaging, anti-counterfeiting and fluorescence sensing. Therefore, the N-CQDs were successfully used for fluorescence turn-off determination of curcumin because of the combined IFE and static quenching process. And a linear relationship in the range of 0.2–8.0 μg/mL and a LOD of 21 ng/mL were realized. And some possible interference compounds including some common cations, sugars, and amino acids and drugs exhibited no evident influence on the detection of curcumin. Furthermore, the developed method was successfully applied for determination of curcumin in human urine samples with low relative standard deviation (RSD) [59]. Another kind of N-CQDs with green emission from the thermal treatment of tartaric acid and urea were also utilized for fluorescence turn-off detection of curcumin based on the combination of IFE and static quenching process, yielding a linear relationship ranging from 0.1 to 20 μM and a LOD of 29.8 nM. Importantly, fourteen amino acids and eight drugs had no obvious impact on curcumin assay, verifying the outstanding selectivity of the N-CQDs towards curcumin. Moreover, the practicality of the fabricated probes was assessed by spiking curcumin in water samples (tap water, mineral water and lake water) and food samples (curry powder, mustard, chili powder, ginger powder and tomato juice), and the results exhibited good recoveries and RSD [60]. Similarly, Dong’s group presented a hydrothermal approach for fabrication of red emissive N-CQDs by using neutral red and ethylenediamine as raw materials. The resultant N-CQDs exhibited good photostability, excellent water solubility and optimal excitation and emission wavelength at 474 nm and 620 nm. Accordingly, the fabricated N-CQDs were utilized for fluorescence turn-off assay of curcumin by virtue of IFE and dynamic quenching process (Fig. 5B). As a result, the quenched fluorescence intensity at 620 nm displayed a good linear relationship with the curcumin concentration in the range of 0.5–35 μM. And the LOD was 76 nM. Furthermore, 15 kinds of amino acids and 5 kinds of drugs showed no significant effect the fluorescence performance of N-CQDs, demonstrating the high selectivity of the proposed method. Moreover, the N-CQDs were also applied for fluorescence turn-off detection of berberine and cell imaging. However, the practicability of the proposed method was not evaluated in real samples and the quantum yield has to been improved [61]. Blue emissive Ag-doped graphite carbon nitride (Ag-g-C3N4) nanosheets were also used for fluorescence turn-off determination of curcumin based on static quenching. As a result, a linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity and the curcumin concentration in the range of 0.01–2.00 μM was established with a LOD of 38 nM. And some metal ions, amino acids and saccharides displayed negligible effects on the curcumin assay. In addition, the Ag-g-C3N4 nanosheets were successfully applied for curcumin detection in human urine and serum samples with good recovery [25].

Recently, dual-element-doped CQDs were also used for fluorescent determination of curcumin. Dong’s group developed a hydrothermal approach for preparation of pH-responsive nitrogen and sulfur-doped CQDs (NS-CQDs) with yellow emission using acid fuchsin as precursor. The as-fabricated NS-CQDs displayed good biocompatibility and excellent photostability. As a result, the NS-CQDs were applied for fluorescence turn-off assay of curcumin due to IFE and electrostatic and hydrogen-bond interaction. The linear relationship ranging from 0.5 μM to 20 μM and a LOD of 81 nM were obtained. And eighteen kinds of metal ions, twenty-two kinds of amino acids and twelve kinds of drugs did not interfere with the detection of curcumin. Furthermore, the proposed approach was successfully used to determine curcumin in urine sample, food and drug with satisfactory results [62]. Another blue-emitting NS-CQDs derived from hydrothermal treatment of sodium citrate and thiourea were also utilized for fluorescence turn-off sensing curcumin based on IFE, yielding a linearity in the range of 0.15–18.0 μM and a LOD of 0.04 μΜ. And the inconspicuous impacts of some ions and small biological molecules on the fluorescence intensity of the NS-CQDs demonstrated the high selectivity of the developed method. In addition, the proposed probe was feasible in detection of curcumin in urine samples [63]. Boron and nitrogen doped CQDs (BN-CQDs) produced from microwave heating possessed blue emission centered at 450 nm and were applied for fluorescence turn-off detection of curcumin via IFE and a dynamic quenching process. As a result, a good performance with linear range of 0.2–12.5 μM and a LOD of 65 nM was obtained. And the possible interferents (metal ions and amino acids) in biological fluids at 20 or 50 μM did not obviously affect the determination of curcumin. Moreover, the developed probes were successfully applied in urine samples with recoveries of 96.5–105.5% [64].

The acid-base neutralization and exothermic carbonization strategy has been also applied for preparation of element-doped CQDs. For example, nitrogen and phosphorus doped CQDs (NP-CQDs) could be fabricated by simply mixing glucose, 1,2-ethylenediamine and concentrated phosphoric acid. The resulted NP-CQDs were utilized for fluorescence turn-off detection of curcumin with a linear range of 0.5–20 µM and a LOD of 58 nM on the basis of IFE. And various amino acids and drugs showed slight impacts on the fluorescence intensity of the NP-CQDs, indicating good selectivity of the proposed method. Furthermore, the detection results in drinking water and food samples demonstrated the feasibility of the proposed method. Moreover, the NP-CQDs displayed low cytotoxicity and were used for cell imaging, revealing the promising applications in biological fields [65]. By virtue of the same strategy, nitrogen and chlorine co-doped CQDs (NCl-CQDs) with blue emission were also synthesized by mixing 1,2-ethylenediamine, glucose and hydrochloric acid. It was found that curcumin could selectively quench the fluorescence of the as-prepared NCl-CQDs due to IFE and a dynamic quenching process (Fig. 6 A). As a result, a linear relationship in the range of 0.1–35 μM and a LOD of 38 nM were achieved. Also, a variety of potential interfering substances, such as various kinds of amino acids, carbohydrates, drugs and cations, had been used to verify the outstanding anti-interference performance of the proposed approach. Furthermore, the practicability of the developed probes was evaluated in food samples [21]. At the same time, nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, and chlorine co-doped CQDs (NSPCl-CQDs) were also constructed by mixing glucose, 1,2-ethylenediamine, 70% H2SO4, concentrated H3PO4 and concentrated HCl. The obtained NSPCl-CQDs exhibited high photostability, good biocompatibility and excellent water solubility. And the fluorescence of NSPCl-CQDs was easily switched off by curcumin due to the combination of electrostatic interaction, IFE, and static quenching process, yielding the selective detection of curcumin with linear ranges of 0.24–13.16 and 13.62–57.79 μM. And the LOD for detection of curcumin was 8.71 nM. A variety of amino acids and some drugs had negligible influence on the assay of curcumin. In addition, the NSPCl-CQDs were successfully applied for sensing curcumin in mustard sauce, curry powder, and sobering turmeric capsule with satisfactory results. Moreover, the NSPCl-CQDs-curcumin were also applied for sensing Eu3+ based on fluorescence recovery [66]. The results demonstrated the potential applications of the resultant NSPCl-CQDs.

Moreover, CQDs could be coated on the surface of (Gd,Eu)2O3-PEI nanospheres via a electrostatic absorption approach. The resultant nanocomposites exhibited blue and red emissions under the excitation of 310 nm. And the addition of curcumin could selectively quench the blue emission of CQDs, but the red emission nearly remained unchanged (Fig. 6B). The phenomenon was ascribed to IFE because the absorption spectrum of curcumin overlapped with the excitation and emission spectra of CQDs, while the reabsorption of curcumin at 400–475 nm preserved the stable red emission. Based on the change of the ratio of two emissions, sensitive detection of curcumin was realized with a linear range of 0.2–3.0 μg/mL and a LOD of 0.0615 μg/mL. However, the selectivity and practical applications of the developed probe were not evaluated [29].
To compare with the performance of these fluorescent nanosensors, we summarized the nanosensor system, sensing strategy, linear range, LOD and real applications in Table 2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Merits of fluorescent nanosensors for curcumin
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the above-mentioned achievements of various nanomaterials for fluorescent sensing curcumin, we can see that there are many merits in the fluorescent nanosensors for curcumin. To better elucidate these advantages, we compared the performance of electrochemical methods, liquid chromatographic methods, organic fluorescent probes and nanosensors. In the electrochemical method, the detection electrode was needed to prepare by using different strategies. For example, carbon nanotube-carboxymethylcellulose electrode has been fabricated to sense curcumin with linear range of 1–48 μM and a LOD of 0.084 μM [67]. The preparation of electrode with good repeatability requires professional operations. In the liquid chromatographic methods, the mobile phase demand organic solvents, which may be not eco-friendly. And complicated operations are also required. For instance, a cold column trapping-cloud point extraction method coupled to high performance liquid chromatography has been proposed for preconcentration and detection of curcumin in human urine. In the method, a nonionic surfactant, ethanol and tetrahydrofurane were demanded, which can cause environmental pollution. And the linear relationship ranging from 0.22 mg/L to 100 mg/L and a LOD of 0.066 mg/L were achieved by the method [68]. In the organic fluorescent probes, complicated organic synthesis and plenty of organic solvents are required. For example, acylhydrazone-bridged bis-tetraphenylethylene (Bis-TPE) has been prepared for fluorescent determination of curcumin based on red-to-green fluorescence change. The detection performance with a linear range of 0–0.5 μM and the LOD of 0.115 μM was obtained. However, the preparation of Bis-TPE involved in three steps of organic synthesis, in which large amounts of organic solvent and complicated operations were required. Furthermore, the as-synthesized Bis-TPE did not totally dissolve in aqueous solution. The use of organic solvent may pollute the environment [19].

For the fluorescent nanosensors, we take NS-CQDs as the example. The NS-CQDs were prepared via the hydrothermal treatment with sodium citrate and thiourea. The as-fabricated NS-CQDs exhibited a high quantum yield of 26.9%, good water solubility and excellent photostability. And the NS-CQDs were utilized for fluorescent detection of curcumin with a linear range of 0.15–18.0 μM and a LOD of 0.04 μM, which were comparable to that of the electrochemical method, liquid chromatographic methods, organic fluorescent probes (Table 1
) [63]. Furthermore, the fluorescent nanosensors displayed excellent water solubility, high quantum yield, ease of fabrication, low-cost and ease of operation, and the preparation, and the use of these nanosensors did not demand organic solvent. These results demonstrated that these nanosensors possessed unique advantages in fluorescent assay of curcumin.

In summary, semiconductor QDs, lanthanide upconversion nanoparticles, fluorescent metal nanoclusters and CQDs have been utilized for fluorescent sensing curcumin. And these advances have been highlighted. However, they are still in infancy for the fluorescent nanosensors of curcumin. Firstly, most of nanosensors were based on fluorescence turn-off mechanism, and the fluorescence intensity was easily affected by other interferences with similar spectrum behavior and the experimental conditions, further yielding unaccurate results. Thus, highly selective nanosensors with fluorescence turn-on or ratiometric response are highly desirable. Secondly, most fluorescent nanosensors were based on CQDs. Other easy-fabricated nanomaterials should be further demonstrated for fluorescent sensing curcumin to expand the nanosensors of curcumin. Thirdly, the practicability of these developed nanosensors was only evaluated by spiking curcumin with real samples. The real practical applications are still further explored to improve the robustness of these developed nanosensors, thus robust fluorescent nanosensors of curcumin are highly desired. Lastly, portable nanosensors of curcumin are also highly desirable owing to simplicity, ease of use and rapidness, realizing real-time on-site detection. With continuous efforts in the exploration of fluorescent nanosensors for curcumin, we do believe that more robust fluorescent nanosensors with good selectivity would be emerged in the coming years.

Yongming Guo: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration. Chao Yang: Writing – original draft. Yijia Zhang: Writing – original draft. Tao Tao: Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was financially supported by the Foundation of NUIST-UoR International Research Institute, the Startup Foundation for Introducing Talent of NUIST (2018r056), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (No. BK20201389), Qing Lan Project of the Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions of China (No. R2018Q03).

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Liu, X. Gong, W. Dong, R. Zhou, S. Shuang, C. Dong, Nitrogen and phosphorus dual-doped carbon dots as a label-free sensor for Curcumin determination in real sample and cellular imaging, Talanta 183 (2018) 61-69.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, and chlorine co-doped carbon nanodots as an “off-on” fluorescent probe for sequential detection of curcumin and europium ion and luxuriant applications
Microchim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Hao, H. Wang, Z. Wang, W. Dong, Q. Hu, S. Shuang, et al., Nitrogen, sulfur, phosphorus, and chlorine co-doped carbon nanodots as an “off-on” fluorescent probe for sequential detection of curcumin and europium ion and luxuriant applications, Microchimica Acta 188 (1) (2021) 16.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical detection of curcumin in food with a carbon nanotube-carboxymethylcellulose electrode
Anal. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Wada, S. Takahashi, H. Muguruma, N. Osakabe, Electrochemical detection of curcumin in food with a carbon nanotube-carboxymethylcellulose electrode, Analytical Sciences 36 (9) (2020) 1113-1118.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cold column trapping-cloud point extraction coupled to high performance liquid chromatography for preconcentration and determination of curcumin in human urine
Anal. Chim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Rahimi, P. Hashemi, F. Nazari, Cold column trapping-cloud point extraction coupled to high performance liquid chromatography for preconcentration and determination of curcumin in human urine, Analytica Chimica Acta 826 (2014) 35-42.
